
---
title: "Lab 4 Instructions"
author: "Your Name Here"
subtitle: "PUBH 523/623: Data Visualization"
date-modified: "today"
format: 
  html:
    link-external-newwindow: true
    toc: true
    self-contained: true
    embed-resources: true
    number-sections: true
    number-depth: 4
    code-annotations: hover
    page-layout: full
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
execute:
  freeze: auto 
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)
library(here)

# Load your summarized data objects from Lab 3

```

### Introduction

This final lab covers data visualization using `ggplot2`. You will map out the relationships between 2-3 project variables simultaneously to produce the core visual anchor for your upcoming final scrollable sheet.

## Lab activities

### Visualizing Distributions with an Asset Lens

To visualize how supportive safety nets or structural assets match up against community stability, you need to construct a plot layout capable of displaying raw data density without losing group visibility.

::: callout-important

#### Task

Create either a boxplot (`geom_boxplot()`) or a violin plot (`geom_violin()`) that charts your continuous asset index on the y-axis against your categorical outcome on the x-axis. Use the `fill` aesthetic to overlay a third variable (such as your demographic group).
:::

### Resolving Overplotting via Jittering

When plotting survey data with distinct integer scores, dots stack directly on top of each other, obscuring sample size density. We will fix this using position adjustments.

::: callout-important

#### Task

Layer jittered raw points over your distribution plot using `geom_jitter()`. If you are filling by a third variable, make sure to use `position = position_jitterdodge()` so the data points align perfectly over their corresponding boxplots. Adjust the `alpha` and `size` parameters to make it readable.
:::

### Adding Professional Design Polish

A public health visualization should communicate clearly without requiring technical code background knowledge from the reader. Clean up the default theme layout, color scales, and title elements.

::: callout-important

#### Task

Incorporate the following styling updates into your plot pipeline:

1. Apply a clean theme function (e.g., `theme_minimal()` or `theme_classic()`).
2. Use an accessible color palette flag (such as `scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")` or a manual viridis palette).
3. Add an asset-focused `labs()` framework containing a clear `title`, `subtitle`, explicit axis labels with units, and a descriptive legend label.
:::

### final Check: Code Styling and Output Verification

Your final scrolling sheet needs to render smoothly without showing messy error blocks or warnings to your audience.

::: callout-important

#### Task

Review your script style according to our workflow guidelines. Set your chunk options (`#| warning: false` and `#| message: false`) appropriately. Render your complete Quarto document to HTML and check your final file output layout.
:::
